dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 for a priority one seizure with a female at a residence in [CITY].
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMS provider. What made you call 911 today?
My mother had a seizure on the couch. It lasted about 7 minutes.
So she had a seizure on the couch?
Yes, the third one today. She seemed fine after it stopped.
Ma'am, I am going to ask you a few questions.
Yes.
Ma'am, what year is it?
I... I think it's May 2009.
Can you squeeze my finger when I say so?
Okay.
She is breathing unassisted, and airway is patent.
Breathing appears unlabored and she's stable at the moment.
I'm going to take her pulse and check her breathing now.
Pulse is 69, strong and regular.
Respirations are 28, and they're normal in depth.
Blood pressure is 133 over 83.
Glucometer reading is 164 mg/dL.
SpO₂ is 98% on room air.
4-lead ECG attached, shows sinus rhythm.
How long has she had this seizure problem?
She has epilepsy, has had a stroke before, and also has dementia with some speech deficits.
She also has hypertension, anxiety, and has been managing her condition.
She's on a variety of medications, including blood pressure and anti-seizure drugs.
She is allergic to sulfonamides, including Bactrim.
On a scale of 0 to 10, what pain is she experiencing?
She didn't seem to be in pain during the seizure. I'd say 0.
So you saw the seizure and called 911, correct?
Yes. She was sitting on the couch when it started.
No bleeding, bruising, or rashes noted.
She still has some tremors, more pronounced on the right than left and in the head and neck area.
The daughter says she didn't appear to have urine leakage during the episode.
She's having tremors in both arms and neck, more on the right side. Her coordination appears minimal.
Hmm... yeah, I can feel it.
Pulse has dropped to 66. Respirations are 20, and they're still normal in depth.
Respirations have decreased to 16, and they're still normal in depth-consistent with improved status.
We will place another 4-lead ECG, just to be safe and monitor any changes in rhythm.
4-lead monitoring on-shows normal sinus rhythm for now.
I see we will need to insert an IV. I'll do it now-okay with you, ma'am?
Yeah, okay.
20g IV in left AC, flushed with 10mL saline, and site appears patent and stable now.
Field impression is seizure and altered mental status. We'll follow the seizure protocol now.
We are going to administer Versed now. She will feel it once we push it into the IV-in a few seconds, she should respond.
2.5mg of midazolam administered via IV push followed by a 10mL saline flush. All went smoothly with no adverse responses observed.
Let's recheck vitals after meds to see if there are any further changes in her status now that she's more alert.
Pulse is now at 63 and stable under observation-showing improvement from earlier readings.
Respirations have decreased to 16, still normal in depth, and no work of breathing noted at this time.
Blood pressure is 135 over 74-consistent with a previously documented baseline.
SpO₂ is at 100%-still above acceptable thresholds on 2LPM oxygen via nasal cannula.
4-lead shows sinus rhythm with a few unifocal PVCs-nothing out of the ordinary as per the ePCR.
Next, normal saline via IV solution-this is for line maintenance and to prepare for transport.
150mL of normal saline administered-tolerated well with no signs of distress or complication.
She responds clearly now and her verbal responses are more coherent. Confusion has decreased post medication as noted in the ePCR.
She is also on atorvastatin, carvedilol, and mirtazapine as part of her cardiac and mental health treatment plan.
Yes, she's always had those, as prescribed. She never misses a dose.
Her other anti-seizure medication, oxcarbazepine, is part of her daily medication regimen.
She is now alert and oriented-able to follow commands. Tremors have subsided as per the medical report after medication.
She's secured on the Reeves chair-ready for transport with no signs of instability.
Transporting to [COMPANY_NAME] Adult ER non-emergently as per medical protocol. Her condition is stable and medication status is under observation.
She's secured in a 5-point harness on the stretcher-transport ready. All straps are in place and the transfer is prepared.
[COMPANY_NAME] is the destination. We'll give a full report to the ED nurse. Nothing life-threatening is observed at this time.
She is currently responsive and oriented. Tremors have subsided completely after medication-post Versed administration, she's stable for transport.
Stroke scale is negative. She is on 2LPM of O₂ via nasal cannula-no further interventions required.
Bilateral upper extremity tremors were noted initially-subsidying after medication.
She is on Levetiracetam, Trileptal, and oxcarbazepine as documented. These align with her documented treatment plan for epilepsy and stroke management.
We will briefly delay for drug box exchange and give the full report to the ED nurse.
Blood pressure is now 137 over 85-baseline consistent with prior readings.
GCS 14 was noted upon arrival, with full alertness and orientation at transfer.
We'll secure the stretcher and optimize transport settings for patient comfort.
[COMPANY_NAME] was selected for care continuity and due to patient's known history and current condition.
All documentation, IV site, and medications are double-checked to ensure a seamless transfer to ED staff.
The IV site is patent and the IV is running freely with the normal saline.
Her baseline has been explained to ED staff, including the nature of her seizures and possible cognitive fluctuation post-episode.
She has no new injuries or symptoms from the episode and remains lucid with no confusion at this time.
She's ready for loading. All restraints have been checked, and we're set for transport.
Transport is set for [COMPANY_NAME] as outlined for patient care.
Patient is secured in stretcher. No changes in patient status during transport preparation.
Administration of IV saline is complete and has not affected her vitals.
She is currently reporting no pain-her level is consistent at 0 out of 10.
No new symptoms reported, and medications have been correctly administered.
She has remained oriented to time, person, and place, and has not exhibited any acute changes.
All gear back in place. Portable monitor secured above the stretcher for transport monitoring.
All set for departure with no pre-hospital interventions pending.
No additional documentation needed. All steps have been captured in the field report.
All restraints and medical equipment are secured and ready. Patient is stable for transport.
Info relayed to dispatch for arrival and arrival reported to receiving hospital in transit.
